title: ML-Powered Enhancement (Evo2HiC)
introduction:
OpenPretext integrates three ML models from the Evo2HiC project that can enhance and predict Hi-C data. These run on a companion server that you start locally — the core app works fully without it.

In this lesson, you'll learn to:
• Connect to the Evo2HiC companion server
• Enhance a low-resolution contact map to reveal hidden structure
• Predict epigenomic tracks (DNase, CTCF, H3K27ac, H3K27me3, H3K4me3) from the contact map
• Predict a Hi-C contact map from DNA sequence alone (Seq2HiC)
• Toggle between original, enhanced, and predicted views
• Understand how enhanced data feeds into downstream analysis

We'll use the King quail genome, a bird with 30 chromosomes and micro-chromosomes — a real-world case where enhancement reveals inter-chromosomal contacts and distance-decay patterns below the noise floor.

Prerequisite: The Evo2HiC server must be running locally. See server/README.md for setup instructions.

steps.title: Prerequisites
steps.instruction:
Before starting, you need the Evo2HiC companion server running locally. In a separate terminal:

```
cd server
uv sync
uv run evo2hic-server
```

The server starts on http://localhost:8000. It works in **mock mode** by default (no model weights needed) — this is fine for learning the workflow. With real weights, the enhancement quality improves dramatically.

Once the server is running, proceed to the next step.
steps.hint: You need Python 3.11+ and the uv package manager installed. The server runs independently from the browser app.

steps.title: Find the Evo2HiC Section
steps.instruction:
Open the sidebar (**I**) and scroll to the bottom of the **3D Analysis** section. Below the standard analysis tools, you'll find the **Evo2HiC** section with:

• A **server URL** field (default: http://localhost:8000)
• A **Check** button to test the connection
• Buttons for enhancement, track prediction, and Hi-C prediction (initially disabled)

The section is self-contained — all server interaction happens here.
steps.hint: If you don't see the Evo2HiC section, scroll further down in the sidebar. It's the last section in 3D Analysis.

steps.title: Connect to the Server
steps.instruction:
Make sure the URL field shows **http://localhost:8000** (the default), then click **Check**.

The status line below the URL will show connection details:
• **OK** — connected successfully
• Device (cpu/mps/cuda) — which hardware is being used
• Model status — whether real models or mock mode are loaded

Once connected, the action buttons become enabled. The server URL is saved to localStorage so you won't need to re-enter it next time.
steps.hint: If the connection fails, verify the server is running (check the terminal for 'Uvicorn running on http://0.0.0.0:8000').

steps.title: Enhance the Contact Map
steps.instruction:
Click **Enhance (Evo2HiC)** to send the overview contact map to the server for resolution enhancement.

The Evo2HiC model (81M parameters, trained on 177 species) enhances the map by:
• Denoising low-signal regions
• Sharpening chromosome boundaries
• Revealing inter-chromosomal contacts hidden in noise
• Restoring distance-decay gradients within chromosomes

A toast notification confirms completion with the processing time. The enhanced map is cached — it won't re-download until you perform a curation operation.
steps.hint: Enhancement typically takes 1–5 seconds depending on map size and hardware. GPU (MPS/CUDA) is much faster than CPU.

steps.title: Toggle Enhanced View
steps.instruction:
Click **Toggle Enhanced View** to switch between the original and enhanced contact maps. Toggle back and forth a few times to see the difference.

In the enhanced view, notice:
• **Chromosome blocks** are sharper and more distinct
• **Distance-decay gradients** within chromosomes become visible (color fading away from diagonal)
• **Inter-chromosomal contacts** between non-adjacent chromosomes may appear as faint off-diagonal signals
• **Noise** in low-coverage regions is reduced

The toggle is a visual switch only — your curation operations always apply to the original contig structure.
steps.hint: The enhanced view is especially useful for identifying subtle misassemblies and chromosome boundaries that are hard to see in the raw data.

steps.title: Enhanced Data in Analysis
steps.instruction:
When the enhanced view is active, downstream analyses automatically use the enhanced data. Try it:

1. Make sure the enhanced view toggle is **on**
2. Click **Compute All** in the 3D Analysis section
3. Compare the health score and P(s) decay with and without enhancement

The enhanced data often produces cleaner analysis results — better P(s) fits, stronger compartment signals, and more accurate TAD boundaries. This is because the model has learned the expected structure from 177 species.

Note: Enhancement is cleared automatically when you perform curation operations (cut, join, invert, move) because the contig ordering changes.
steps.hint: Toggle the enhanced view off and re-run Compute All to see the difference in health scores between raw and enhanced data.

steps.title: Predict Epigenomic Tracks
steps.instruction:
Click **Predict Tracks** to predict 5 epigenomic tracks from the contact map:

• **DNase** (blue) — DNase I hypersensitivity, marks open chromatin
• **CTCF** (orange) — CTCF binding, marks insulator/boundary elements
• **H3K27ac** (green) — active enhancers and promoters
• **H3K27me3** (red) — Polycomb-repressed regions
• **H3K4me3** (purple) — active promoters

These tracks appear along the contact map edges, just like insulation or compartment tracks. For non-model organisms like King quail where no ChIP-seq data exists, predicted tracks provide useful chromatin context for curation decisions.

Toggle tracks on with **X** if they aren't visible.
steps.hint: The predicted tracks are per-bin values scaled to [0,1]. High values indicate strong predicted signal at that genomic position.

steps.title: Explore Predicted Tracks
steps.instruction:
Zoom into a large chromosome block to examine the predicted tracks in detail. Look for patterns:

• **DNase peaks** tend to cluster at TAD boundaries and active regions
• **CTCF** often marks the same boundaries as insulation score dips
• **H3K27ac** and **H3K4me3** correlate with active A compartments
• **H3K27me3** is enriched in B compartments (repressed regions)

Compare the predicted epigenomic tracks with the A/B compartment track (if computed). Active marks (DNase, H3K27ac, H3K4me3) should be enriched in A compartments, while H3K27me3 should be enriched in B compartments.

You can configure track visibility, colors, and rendering mode via the Tracks panel in the sidebar.
steps.hint: Click on the track name in the Tracks sidebar section to expand its settings (color, mode, visibility).

steps.title: Load a FASTA File (for Seq2HiC)
steps.instruction:
The Seq2HiC model predicts Hi-C contact maps from DNA sequence alone. To use it, you need to load a reference FASTA file first.

Click the **Load FASTA** button in the toolbar (or drag-drop a .fasta/.fa file). Once loaded, a hint below the Evo2HiC buttons shows the number of sequences loaded.

If you don't have a FASTA file available, you can skip this step and the next one — Seq2HiC requires real FASTA sequences and is not available with the synthetic demo data.
steps.hint: FASTA files for GenomeArk specimens can be downloaded from the same S3 bucket as the .pretext files.

steps.title: Predict Hi-C from Sequence (Seq2HiC)
steps.instruction:
With FASTA sequences loaded, click **Predict Hi-C (Seq2HiC)** to predict what the Hi-C contact map should look like based on DNA sequence alone.

This is a powerful comparison tool:
• If observed and predicted maps agree, the assembly structure is consistent with the sequence
• Discrepancies may indicate misassemblies, structural variants, or regions where the Hi-C library has biases

After prediction completes, the **Toggle Predicted vs Observed** button becomes active.
steps.hint: Seq2HiC requires FASTA sequences. The button stays disabled if no sequences are loaded.

steps.title: Compare Predicted vs Observed
steps.instruction:
Click **Toggle Predicted vs Observed** to switch between the observed Hi-C map and the sequence-predicted map.

Compare the two views:
• Both should show similar chromosome block structure if the assembly is correct
• Differences in block boundaries might indicate misassembly
• The predicted map won't show library-specific artifacts (restriction site biases, PCR duplicates)

This toggle is independent of the enhancement toggle — you can switch between original, enhanced, and sequence-predicted views.
steps.hint: The predicted map is generated at overview resolution, matching the size of the overview contact map.

steps.title: Session Persistence
steps.instruction:
All Evo2HiC outputs are saved when you save a session (Cmd+S or the Session section in the sidebar):

• **Enhanced map** — the full enhanced overview is stored as base64
• **Predicted tracks** — all 5 epigenomic tracks with their values and colors
• **Predicted Hi-C** — the sequence-predicted contact map

When you reload the session, these are restored without needing the server. This means you can run the server once, generate all predictions, save, and then work offline.

Note: Curation operations (cut, join, invert, move) invalidate the enhanced map and predicted Hi-C because the contig ordering changes. Predicted epigenomic tracks are also cleared since they correspond to the pre-curation map layout.
steps.hint: Save a session after running all three predictions to avoid re-computing them later.

steps.title: Evo2HiC Summary
steps.instruction:
You've learned the three ML capabilities in OpenPretext:

1. **Enhancement** — denoises and sharpens the contact map, revealing hidden structure
2. **Epigenomic Track Prediction** — predicts 5 chromatin marks (DNase, CTCF, H3K27ac, H3K27me3, H3K4me3) from the Hi-C map alone
3. **Seq2HiC** — predicts what the Hi-C map should look like from DNA sequence

These tools are most valuable for:
• **Non-model organisms** with no existing ChIP-seq or epigenomic data
• **Low-coverage assemblies** where the raw map is noisy
• **Quality control** — comparing observed vs sequence-predicted structure
• **Analysis improvement** — enhanced data produces cleaner TAD calls and compartment signals

The server runs in mock mode for testing and with real Evo2HiC weights (81M parameters, trained on 177 species) for production use. See server/README.md for instructions on downloading model weights.
